walkthrough.title: Choose Your Tribe
walkthrough.text: Let's pick our tribes! Skull comes with six beautifully illustrated tribe sets. Each player chooses one tribe and takes the matching play mat and four discs. The discs have gorgeous artwork on them, and each set has its own unique style.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Discs
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what we got. Each player has exactly four discs: three flowers and one skull. The flowers are your friends and the skull is your weapon. Keep all four discs hidden in your hand so nobody can see which is which.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Play Mat
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up our mats. Place your play mat in front of you with the skull side facing up. This is the starting position. When you win your first challenge, you will flip it to the flower side. Win a second challenge and you win the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Choose a First Player
walkthrough.text: Let's pick who goes first. Choose any method you like. Being first player is actually a real advantage in Skull because you control the pace of the round. The first player will kick off every round by placing the first disc.

summary.title: Choose Your Tribe
bullets: Each player picks 1 of 6 tribe sets
Take the matching play mat + 4 discs

summary.title: Know Your Discs
bullets: 3 flower discs + 1 skull disc per player
Keep all discs hidden in hand

summary.title: Place Your Play Mat
bullets: Mat starts skull-side up
Flip to flower after 1st win → flip again (2nd win) = victory

summary.title: Choose a First Player
bullets: Any method to select first player
First player advantage: controls round pacing

walkthrough.title: Round Overview
walkthrough.text: Every round has three steps. First, everyone places one disc face down. Second, players take turns either adding more discs or starting a challenge. Third, the challenger flips discs to try to find only flowers. That is the whole game.

walkthrough.title: Step One: Everyone Places a Disc
walkthrough.text: Nobody reveals what they placed. This is where the bluffing begins. Did you place a flower or your skull? Only you know.

walkthrough.title: Step Two: Add or Challenge
walkthrough.text: Starting with the first player and going clockwise, each player has two choices on their turn. You can add another disc face down on top of your stack. Or you can start a challenge by announcing a number. Once someone challenges, no more discs can be added.

walkthrough.title: How Bidding Works
walkthrough.text: When someone starts a challenge, they announce how many total discs they think they can flip without hitting a skull. Going clockwise, each other player must either raise the bid or pass. If you pass, slide your mat forward to show you are out. The last player left bidding is the challenger.

walkthrough.title: The Attempt
walkthrough.text: The challenger must flip discs equal to their bid. Here is the critical rule: you must flip all of your own discs first before touching anyone else's stack. After your own stack, you choose whose discs to flip in any order, always from the top down.

walkthrough.title: Success and Failure
walkthrough.text: If the challenger flips their bid number without revealing a skull, they succeed. Flip your mat to the flower side. If the mat is already on the flower side, you win the game. If a skull appears at any point, stop immediately. The attempt has failed.

walkthrough.title: Failed Attempt Penalty
walkthrough.text: When an attempt fails, all players take their discs back. The challenger must permanently lose one disc. They shuffle their remaining discs face down, and the player whose skull was revealed chooses which disc to remove without looking at it. If you revealed your own skull, you choose which disc to lose.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: There are two ways to win Skull. First, succeed at two challenges. Your mat tracks this by flipping from skull side to flower side after your first win. Second, be the last player standing if everyone else has been eliminated. You get eliminated when you lose your last disc.

summary.title: Round Overview
bullets: 3 steps: place 1 disc → add/challenge → attempt
Pure bluffing — no luck, no hidden information except disc identity

bullets: All players simultaneously place 1 disc face-down on mat
Flower or skull — only you know

summary.title: Step Two: Add or Challenge
bullets: Clockwise from first player: add 1 disc OR start a challenge
Once bidding starts, no more adding

Choose opponent stack order freely

Already flower side + success = WIN
Any skull = immediate failure, stop flipping

Challenger loses 1 disc permanently (random, chosen by skull owner)

summary.title: Winning the Game
bullets: Win condition 1: succeed at 2 challenges
Win condition 2: last player not eliminated

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Bluff
walkthrough.text: We will walk through a practice round with four players. Skull is all about reading people and bluffing, so pay attention to what everyone does and says. Let's deal out the tribes and get started.

walkthrough.title: Everyone Places Their First Disc
walkthrough.text: Let's begin. Each of our four players secretly picks one disc and places it face down on their mat. Player one places a flower. Player two places a flower. Player three places their skull, grinning innocently. Player four places a flower. Nobody knows what anyone else placed.

walkthrough.title: Adding Phase Begins
walkthrough.text: Player one starts. They could challenge, but with only four discs on the table, the risk is high. Instead, they add a second flower disc on top of their first. Player two also adds a flower. Player three adds a flower on top of their skull, hiding it deeper. Player four adds another flower.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Starts a Challenge
walkthrough.text: Now we have eight discs on the table. Player four feels confident and starts a challenge: they bid three. That means they think they can flip three discs without hitting a skull. The bidding war begins.

walkthrough.title: Bidding Heats Up
walkthrough.text: Player one raises to four. Player two passes and slides their mat forward. Player three raises to five. Player four passes. Player one thinks about it and passes too. Player three is now the challenger with a bid of five.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Forgetting Own Discs
walkthrough.text: Player three bids five, feeling great. But wait. Player three must flip all of their own discs first before touching anyone else's. Their own stack has a skull buried underneath a flower. They flip their top disc, a flower. Then they flip the second disc and it is their own skull. The attempt fails immediately.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check Your Own Stack
walkthrough.text: This is the number one beginner mistake in Skull. You must flip all of your own discs first. If you placed a skull in your stack, you will reveal it before you ever get to choose opponents' discs. Never bid higher than you can safely cover after flipping your entire stack. Count your own safe discs before bidding.

walkthrough.title: Penalty Applied
walkthrough.text: Since Player three flipped their own skull, they choose which disc to lose. They shuffle their remaining discs face down and pick one to remove from the game without looking at it. Player three now has only three discs. They are still in the game, but with fewer options.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Begins
walkthrough.text: Player three is the new first player because they were the challenger. Everyone places one disc face down again. This time, Player three has learned their lesson and places a flower. Everyone else places a disc secretly too.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Mistake: Tunnel Vision
walkthrough.text: After some adding, Player two becomes the challenger with a bid of four. They flip their own two discs safely, both flowers. Then they pick Player one's stack and start flipping. Top disc is a flower. They keep going into the same stack, and the second disc is a skull. Player two could have switched to a different player's stack after one disc.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spread Your Reveals
walkthrough.text: Do not tunnel into one opponent's stack. After clearing your own discs, pick the safest-looking stacks and only take one or two from each. The deeper you dig into a single stack, the more likely you are to hit a buried skull. Spread your reveals across multiple players to reduce risk.

walkthrough.title: Player One Wins a Challenge
walkthrough.text: In the next round, Player one makes a conservative bid of two. They flip their own flower, then carefully pick the top disc from Player four's single-disc stack. It is a flower. Success! Player one flips their mat from skull side to flower side. One more win and they take the game.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen a full practice round of Skull. You watched Player three learn the hard way about checking their own stack, and Player two learn about spreading reveals across multiple stacks. Remember: always know what is in your own stack before bidding, and never dig too deep into one opponent's pile.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Bluff
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your real game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for bluffing strategies and reading opponents.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick rundown of all the terms and rules.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Ready to Bluff

Reference the Appendix → quick terms and rules
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: The Art of the Bluff
walkthrough.text: Skull is a pure bluffing game, and the best bluffs are consistent. If you always hesitate before placing your skull, opponents will read you like a book. Practice placing every disc with the same timing and body language. The goal is to make your skull placement look identical to your flower placement.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Watch how other players behave when they place discs and when they bid. Do they bid aggressively after placing a flower? Do they go quiet after placing their skull? Everyone has tells, and finding them is half the fun. Pay special attention to how quickly someone passes during bidding.

walkthrough.title: Conservative Bidding Wins
walkthrough.text: New players often bid too high too fast. A bid of two or three is much safer than trying to be a hero with five or six. You only need two successful challenges to win the whole game. Winning small and steady beats one spectacular failure that costs you a disc.

walkthrough.title: When to Place Your Skull
walkthrough.text: Your skull is a trap for other players. Place it when you think someone else is about to challenge. The ideal moment is when you expect to be passed over during reveals. If you are the first player and you want to challenge this round, place a flower so your own stack is safe.

walkthrough.title: Disc Count Awareness
walkthrough.text: Always count the total discs on the table and track how many discs each player has remaining. A player with only one or two discs is more predictable. If someone has already lost discs, they might not even have their skull anymore, making their stack safer to reveal.

walkthrough.title: The Power of Passing
walkthrough.text: Passing during bidding is not a sign of weakness. Sometimes letting someone else take the risk is the smartest play. If you placed your skull and someone else is about to become the challenger, passing and watching them hit your skull is a great way to eliminate their discs.

walkthrough.title: Using Table Talk
walkthrough.text: Skull thrives on table talk. Comment on other players' stacks, question their confidence, and try to push them into bad bids. Just remember that everything you say is also a signal. If you talk too much about one player's stack, you might be steering the challenger away from your own skull.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Awareness
walkthrough.text: When a player has their mat on the flower side, they are one challenge away from winning. Focus your skull placement to target that player. If you are the one at match point, keep your bids conservative. You do not need a flashy win. You just need any successful challenge.

summary.title: The Art of the Bluff
bullets: Place every disc with the same timing and body language
Skull placement should look identical to flower placement
Consistency is the best disguise

summary.title: Reading Your Opponents
bullets: Watch behavior during disc placement and bidding
Spot tells: hesitation, aggression, silence
Pay attention to how quickly someone passes

bullets: Bids of 2-3 are safer than heroic 5-6
Only need 2 successful challenges to win
Steady small wins > one spectacular failure

Skull = trap for opponents, not for yourself

Let others take the risk when your skull is out

summary.title: Using Table Talk

Everything you say is also information about you
Steer challengers toward or away from stacks

summary.title: Endgame Awareness
bullets: Target players at match point (flower-side mat) with skulls
At match point yourself → bid conservatively
Any successful challenge wins — no need for flash

walkthrough.title: The Self-Challenge Bluff
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest plays in Skull is placing your skull and then challenging low yourself. You bid one, flip your skull immediately, and lose a disc on purpose. Why? Because opponents see you challenging after placing and assume you always place flowers before challenging. Next round, do it again but with a flower. They will expect the skull and steer clear of your stack.

walkthrough.title: Baiting High Bids
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about forcing opponents into dangerous positions. Add discs quickly and confidently to build a tall stack. Other players see a big stack and assume it is full of flowers. When they bid high to include your discs, your buried skull catches them. The key is selling the confidence.

walkthrough.title: Disc Economy Management
walkthrough.text: Every disc you lose narrows your options. A player with four discs can bluff freely. A player with two discs is an open book. Protect your discs by avoiding reckless bids and placing skulls strategically. Sometimes it is worth passing on a potential win to avoid the risk of losing a disc.

walkthrough.title: The Guaranteed Challenge
walkthrough.text: If you placed only flowers and you are bidding for exactly the number of discs in your own stack, you cannot fail because you must flip your own discs first. Use this when you are at match point to guarantee a win if nobody raises the bid past your stack size.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Meta
walkthrough.text: After several rounds, patterns emerge. Track who plays aggressively, who bluffs often, and who always plays safe. Use this information to predict disc placements. If someone always places their skull on round one, they will eventually realize you know and switch. Adapt faster than your opponents.

walkthrough.title: Player Elimination Tactics
walkthrough.text: When an opponent is down to one or two discs, you can apply targeted pressure. Bid low to become the challenger and reveal their stack specifically. If they have one disc and it is their skull, they are eliminated. The psychological pressure of near-elimination often causes panicked mistakes.

walkthrough.title: Multiplayer Dynamics
walkthrough.text: In games with five or six players, information is noisier and bluffs are harder to read. Use the chaos to your advantage. With more players, challenges are riskier because there are more skulls on the table. Bid conservatively and let aggressive players eliminate each other while you preserve your discs.

walkthrough.title: The Double Bluff
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about advanced deception. Announce your intention to challenge, then place your skull. When bidding starts, immediately raise aggressively. Opponents will assume you would never place a skull if you planned to challenge. When someone else wins the bidding, they will likely flip your stack early, thinking it is safe. This is the double bluff, and it is devastatingly effective once you have established a pattern.

summary.title: The Self-Challenge Bluff
bullets: Place skull → challenge low → fail on purpose → lose a disc
Opponents now assume you always place flowers when challenging
Next round, challenge with a flower and opponents avoid your stack

summary.title: Baiting High Bids
bullets: Build tall stacks confidently to lure high bids
Buried skull catches challengers who include your stack
Confidence is the key to selling the bait

summary.title: Disc Economy Management
bullets: 4 discs = full bluffing range; 2 discs = predictable
Protect discs over chasing wins
Sometimes passing > risking disc loss

summary.title: The Guaranteed Challenge
bullets: Bid exactly your own stack size with all flowers placed
You flip only your own discs → guaranteed success
Use at match point to close out the game

summary.title: Reading the Meta

entries.definition: One of three safe discs in your hand. Revealing a flower during a challenge is a success. Flowers are your tools for building safe stacks.
Your single dangerous disc. If revealed during any challenge, the attempt fails immediately. Used as a trap for opponents or a bluff tool.
Your personal board with two sides. Starts skull-side up. Flips to flower-side after your first successful challenge. A second success wins the game.
A bid to reveal a certain number of discs from all mats without finding a skull. The challenger is the last player remaining in the bidding war.
Place one disc from your hand face down on top of your stack during the adding phase. Offset discs so the stack height is visible to all players.
Decline to raise the bid during the challenge phase. Slide your mat forward to indicate you are out of bidding. Your discs remain on the table and can still be flipped.
The phase where the challenger flips discs. Must flip all own discs first, then choose opponents' stacks in any order, always revealing top disc first.
A challenge where a skull is revealed. The challenger permanently loses one disc, chosen by the skull owner. All discs return to hands.
A challenge where the bid number of flowers is revealed with no skulls. The challenger flips their mat. If already on flower side, they win the game.
When a player loses their last disc, they are removed from the game. Their mat and remaining components are set aside.
The player who leads each round by placing the first disc and having the first option to add or challenge. The challenger becomes first player of the next round.
The pile of face-down discs on a player's mat. Discs are offset so the count is visible. Reveals always happen from the top down.
The number of discs a player claims they can reveal without hitting a skull. Must be at least one and cannot exceed the total discs on all mats.
